question: 2. Prove that if the points in the complex plane corresponding to two distinct complex numbers \( z_{1} \) and \( z_{2} \) are two vertices of an equilateral triangle, then the third vertex corresponds to \( -\omega z_{1}-\omega^{2} z_{2} \), where \( \omega \) is an imaginary cube root of unity.
solution:
Solution. If the complex numbers \( z_{1}, z_{2}, z_{3} \) are the vertices of an equilateral triangle, then
\[
\frac{z_{3}-z_{1}}{z_{2}-z_{1}}=\alpha=e^{ \pm \pi i / 3}
\]

Hence \( z_{3}=(1-\alpha) z_{1}+\alpha z_{2}=-\alpha^{2} z_{1}-\alpha^{4} z_{2} \), because \( \alpha^{2}-\alpha+1=0 \) and \( \alpha^{3}=-1 \) for either determination of \( \alpha \). Thus
\[
z_{3}=-\omega z_{1}-\omega^{2} z_{2}
\]
where \( \omega=\alpha^{2}=e^{+2 \pi i / 3} \) is one of the two complex cube roots of unity.
Note that given \( z_{1} \) and \( z_{2} \) there are two ways to complete an equilateral triangle, corresponding to the two choices for \( \omega \).

descriptive_long.question: 2. Prove that if the points in the complex plane corresponding to two distinct complex numbers \( firstvertex \) and \( secondvertex \) are two vertices of an equilateral triangle, then the third vertex corresponds to \( -cuberoot firstvertex-cuberoot^{2} secondvertex \), where \( cuberoot \) is an imaginary cube root of unity.
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. If the complex numbers \( firstvertex, secondvertex, thirdvertex \) are the vertices of an equilateral triangle, then
\[
\frac{thirdvertex-firstvertex}{secondvertex-firstvertex}=rotationfactor=e^{ \pm \pi i / 3}
\]

Hence \( thirdvertex=(1-rotationfactor) firstvertex+rotationfactor secondvertex=-rotationfactor^{2} firstvertex-rotationfactor^{4} secondvertex \), because \( rotationfactor^{2}-rotationfactor+1=0 \) and \( rotationfactor^{3}=-1 \) for either determination of \( rotationfactor \). Thus
\[
thirdvertex=-cuberoot firstvertex-cuberoot^{2} secondvertex
\]
where \( cuberoot=rotationfactor^{2}=e^{+2 \pi i / 3} \) is one of the two complex cube roots of unity.
Note that given \( firstvertex \) and \( secondvertex \) there are two ways to complete an equilateral triangle, corresponding to the two choices for \( cuberoot \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question: 2. Prove that if the points in the complex plane corresponding to two distinct complex numbers \( marshmallow \) and \( lighthouse \) are two vertices of an equilateral triangle, then the third vertex corresponds to \( -blueberries\, marshmallow-blueberries^{2}\, lighthouse \), where blueberries is an imaginary cube root of unity.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. If the complex numbers \( marshmallow, lighthouse, teacupset \) are the vertices of an equilateral triangle, then
\[
\frac{teacupset-marshmallow}{lighthouse-marshmallow}=thundersnow=e^{ \pm \pi i / 3}
\]
Hence \( teacupset=(1-thundersnow)\, marshmallow+thundersnow\, lighthouse=-thundersnow^{2}\, marshmallow-thundersnow^{4}\, lighthouse \), because \( thundersnow^{2}-thundersnow+1=0 \) and \( thundersnow^{3}=-1 \) for either determination of thundersnow. Thus
\[
teacupset=-blueberries\, marshmallow-blueberries^{2}\, lighthouse
\]
where \( blueberries=thundersnow^{2}=e^{+2 \pi i / 3} \) is one of the two complex cube roots of unity.
Note that given marshmallow and lighthouse there are two ways to complete an equilateral triangle, corresponding to the two choices for blueberries.

descriptive_long_misleading.question: 2. Prove that if the points in the complex plane corresponding to two distinct complex numbers \( linepoint \) and \( planepoint \) are two vertices of an equilateral triangle, then the third vertex corresponds to \( -chaosfactor linepoint-chaosfactor^{2} planepoint \), where \( chaosfactor \) is an imaginary cube root of unity.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. If the complex numbers \( linepoint, planepoint, voidspace \) are the vertices of an equilateral triangle, then
\[
\frac{voidspace-linepoint}{planepoint-linepoint}=stillfactor=e^{ \pm \pi i / 3}
\]

Hence \( voidspace=(1-stillfactor) linepoint+stillfactor planepoint=-stillfactor^{2} linepoint-stillfactor^{4} planepoint \), because \( stillfactor^{2}-stillfactor+1=0 \) and \( stillfactor^{3}=-1 \) for either determination of \( stillfactor \). Thus
\[
voidspace=-chaosfactor linepoint-chaosfactor^{2} planepoint
\]
where \( chaosfactor=stillfactor^{2}=e^{+2 \pi i / 3} \) is one of the two complex cube roots of unity.
Note that given \( linepoint \) and \( planepoint \) there are two ways to complete an equilateral triangle, corresponding to the two choices for \( chaosfactor \).

garbled_string.question: 2. Prove that if the points in the complex plane corresponding to two distinct complex numbers \( qzxwvtnp \) and \( hjgrksla \) are two vertices of an equilateral triangle, then the third vertex corresponds to \( -fljgksnd qzxwvtnp-fljgksnd^{2} hjgrksla \), where \( fljgksnd \) is an imaginary cube root of unity.
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. If the complex numbers \( qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla, nbvcklqe \) are the vertices of an equilateral triangle, then
\[
\frac{nbvcklqe-qzxwvtnp}{hjgrksla-qzxwvtnp}=pwszmrtd=e^{ \pm \pi i / 3}
\]
Hence \( nbvcklqe=(1-pwszmrtd) qzxwvtnp+pwszmrtd hjgrksla=-pwszmrtd^{2} qzxwvtnp-pwszmrtd^{4} hjgrksla \), because \( pwszmrtd^{2}-pwszmrtd+1=0 \) and \( pwszmrtd^{3}=-1 \) for either determination of \( pwszmrtd \). Thus
\[
nbvcklqe=-fljgksnd qzxwvtnp-fljgksnd^{2} hjgrksla
\]
where \( fljgksnd=pwszmrtd^{2}=e^{+2 \pi i / 3} \) is one of the two complex cube roots of unity.
Note that given \( qzxwvtnp \) and \( hjgrksla \) there are two ways to complete an equilateral triangle, corresponding to the two choices for \( fljgksnd \).

kernel_variant.question:
Let  
\[
\varepsilon:=e^{-\pi i/3}= \tfrac12-\tfrac{\sqrt3}{2}i,
\qquad
\rho:=\varepsilon-1=e^{-2\pi i/3},
\]
so that $\rho^{3}=1$ and $\rho\neq1$.  
For two distinct complex numbers $(z_{0},z_{1})$ define the (clockwise)  
equilateral-completion map  
\[
E(z_{0},z_{1})\;:=\;(1-\varepsilon)\,z_{0}\;+\;\varepsilon\,z_{1}.\tag{$\star$}
\]

(The point $E(z_{0},z_{1})$ is the unique third vertex that completes
the clockwise-oriented equilateral triangle on the ordered side
$z_{0}z_{1}$.)  

Introduce the linear transformation  
\[
\Phi:\Bbb C^{2}\longrightarrow\Bbb C^{2},\qquad
\Phi(z_{0},z_{1})=(z_{1},E(z_{0},z_{1})).
\]

Starting from a non-degenerate ordered edge $(z_{0},z_{1})$ consider the
sequence of vertices  
\[
z_{n+1}\;=\;E(z_{\,n-1},z_{\,n})\qquad(n\ge 1).\tag{$\clubsuit$}
\]
(The ordered pairs $(z_{n-1},z_{n})$ arise by iterating $\Phi$.)

Answer the following questions.

1.  Algebraic background  
    (a)  Prove that $E$ is $\Bbb C$-linear in the ordered pair
         $(z_{0},z_{1})$, and determine the matrix $A$ of $\Phi$ with
         respect to the standard basis of $\Bbb C^{2}$.  
    (b)  Compute the characteristic and the minimal polynomial of $A$,
         prove that $A$ has the distinct eigenvalues $1$ and $\rho$,
         deduce that $A$ is diagonalisable and satisfies $A^{3}=I$
         while $A\neq I$ and $A^{2}\neq I$.  
         Conclude that $\Phi$ has exact order $3$.

2.  Explicit dynamics  
    (a)  Put $w:=z_{1}-z_{0}$.  Show that for every $n\ge0$
         \[
           z_{n}
           \;=\;
           z_{0}\;+\;\frac{1-\rho^{\,n}}{1-\rho}\,w.
         \]
    (b)  Conclude that $(z_{n})$ is $3$-periodic and that the orbit is
         constant if and only if $z_{1}=z_{0}$.

3.  Geometry  
    (a)  Verify directly from $(\star)$ that
         $\Delta_{0}:=(z_{0},z_{1},z_{2})$ is a clockwise-oriented
         equilateral triangle.  
    (b)  Let
         \[
           G\;:=\;\frac{z_{0}+z_{1}+z_{2}}{3}
         \]
         be its centroid.  Prove the fundamental rotation identity
         \[
           z_{n+1}-G
           \;=\;
           \rho\,(z_{n}-G)\qquad(n\ge0),
         \]
         and deduce that $\Phi$ acts, on single vertices, as the
         rotation of order $3$ about $G$ through the angle
         $\arg\rho=-\tfrac{2\pi}{3}$.  Determine, in terms of $|w|$,  
           * the circum-radius $R$, and  
           * the in-radius $r$  
         of $\Delta_{0}$.  (Show in particular that $R\neq r$ although
         both centres coincide at $G$.)  
    (c)  Conversely, show that every clockwise-oriented equilateral
         triangle in $\Bbb C$ arises from a unique ordered side
         $(z_{0},z_{1})$ via the scheme $(\clubsuit)$.

4.  Arithmetic refinement over the Eisenstein integers  
   Let $\omega:=e^{2\pi i/3}$ and $\mathfrak E:=\Bbb Z[\omega]$.

   (a)  Prove that $\varepsilon=-\omega$ and $1-\varepsilon=1+\omega$
        both lie in $\mathfrak E$ and deduce that
        $E(\mathfrak E,\mathfrak E)\subset\mathfrak E$; hence for
        $z_{0},z_{1}\in\mathfrak E$ the whole $3$-periodic orbit
        $(z_{0},z_{1},z_{2})$ lies in $\mathfrak E$.  Exhibit an explicit
        example with exactly one of $z_{0},z_{1}$ in $\mathfrak E$
        for which $z_{2}\notin\mathfrak E$.  

   (b)  From now on assume that the three vertices themselves lie in
        $\mathfrak E$.  Fix a non-zero $d\in\mathfrak E$.  
        Up to translation by elements of $\mathfrak E$, determine and
        count all clockwise-oriented equilateral triangles whose first
        side-vector equals $d$.  Explain how the unit group
        $\{\pm1,\pm\omega,\pm\omega^{2}\}$ acts on this family.



%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
kernel_variant.solution:
We keep the notation  
\[
\varepsilon=e^{-\pi i/3},\qquad
\rho=\varepsilon-1=e^{-2\pi i/3},\qquad
w:=z_{1}-z_{0}.
\]

\textbf{1. Algebraic background}

(a)  For $(z_{0},z_{1}),(u_{0},u_{1})\in\Bbb C^{2}$ and
$\lambda\in\Bbb C$ we have
\[
E(\lambda z_{0}+u_{0},\lambda z_{1}+u_{1})
=(1-\varepsilon)(\lambda z_{0}+u_{0})
+\varepsilon(\lambda z_{1}+u_{1})
=\lambda E(z_{0},z_{1})+E(u_{0},u_{1}),
\]
so $E$ is \emph{linear} (not bilinear) in the ordered pair.  
Writing column vectors $v=(z_{0},z_{1})^{\mathsf T}$ we obtain
\[
\Phi(v)=\bigl(z_{1},(1-\varepsilon)z_{0}+\varepsilon z_{1}\bigr)^{\mathsf T}
=A\,v
\quad\text{with}\quad
A=\begin{bmatrix}0&1\\ 1-\varepsilon&\varepsilon\end{bmatrix}.
\]

(b)  The characteristic polynomial is
\[
\chi_{A}(\lambda)=
\det(\lambda I-A)=\lambda^{2}-\varepsilon\lambda-(1-\varepsilon).
\]
Since $\chi_{A}(1)=0$ and $\chi_{A}(\rho)=0$, we factor
\[
\chi_{A}(\lambda)=(\lambda-1)(\lambda-\rho).
\]
The two roots are distinct, hence $A$ is diagonalisable and the minimal
polynomial equals $\chi_{A}$.  Because $\rho^{3}=1$ we have
$\lambda^{3}-1=(\lambda-1)(\lambda-\rho)(\lambda-\rho^{2})$, thus
$\chi_{A}\mid(\lambda^{3}-1)$ and $A^{3}=I$.  Since $\rho\neq\pm1$,
neither $A$ nor $A^{2}$ equals $I$, so $\Phi$ has exact order $3$.

\medskip
\textbf{2. Explicit dynamics}

Let $v_{n}:=(z_{\,n-1},z_{\,n})^{\mathsf T}$ for $n\ge1$.  Then
$v_{n}=A^{\,n-1}v_{1}$.  Diagonalising $A$ gives
\[
A^{n}=P_{1}+\rho^{\,n}P_{\rho},
\qquad
P_{1}+P_{\rho}=I,
\qquad
P_{1}P_{\rho}=0,
\]
where $P_{1},P_{\rho}$ are the eigen-projectors.
Applying this to $v_{1}$ and taking the second coordinate yields
\[
z_{n}=z_{0}+\frac{1-\rho^{\,n}}{1-\rho}\,w\qquad(n\ge0).
\]
Because $\rho^{3}=1$, the sequence $(z_{n})$ is $3$-periodic.  It is
constant iff $w=0$, i.e. $z_{1}=z_{0}$.

\medskip
\textbf{3. Geometry}

(a)  From $(\star)$ we compute
\[
z_{2}-z_{0}=E(z_{0},z_{1})-z_{0}=\varepsilon w,\qquad
z_{2}-z_{1}=\rho w.
\]
Hence all three side lengths equal $|w|$ and the oriented angle from
$z_{1}-z_{0}$ to $z_{2}-z_{1}$ equals $\arg\rho=-\tfrac{2\pi}{3}$,
so $\Delta_{0}$ is clockwise equilateral.

(b)  Put $G:=(z_{0}+z_{1}+z_{2})/3$.  Using the explicit formula for
$z_{n}$ with $n=0,1,2$ we find
\[
z_{1}-G=\rho\,(z_{0}-G),\qquad
z_{2}-G=\rho\,(z_{1}-G),
\]
and by induction
$z_{n+1}-G=\rho\,(z_{n}-G)$ for all $n\ge0$.  Hence $\Phi$
acts as the rotation about $G$ through the angle $-120^{\circ}$.

Because every vertex is at distance
\[
R=|z_{0}-G|=\frac{|w|}{\sqrt3}
\]
from $G$, this distance is the circum-radius.  In an equilateral
triangle the in-radius is $r=R/2$, i.e.
\[
r=\frac{|w|}{2\sqrt3},
\qquad
\text{so}\quad R\neq r.
\]

(c)  Conversely, given a clockwise equilateral triangle
$T=\{p,q,r\}$ with ordered side $(p,q)$, the definition of $E$
forces $r=E(p,q)$; iteration of $(\clubsuit)$ therefore reproduces
$T$.  Different ordered sides give different triangles, establishing
a bijection.

\medskip
\textbf{4. Arithmetic refinement over $\mathfrak E=\Bbb Z[\omega]$}

(a)  We have $\varepsilon=-\omega$ and $1-\varepsilon=1+\omega\in\mathfrak
E$, hence $E(\mathfrak E,\mathfrak E)\subset\mathfrak E$ and any orbit
starting in $\mathfrak E^{2}$ remains in $\mathfrak E$ by
$3$-periodicity.  As a counter-example with only one entry in
$\mathfrak E$ take
\[
z_{0}=\tfrac12\notin\mathfrak E,\qquad
z_{1}=0\in\mathfrak E.
\]
Then
\[
z_{2}=E(z_{0},z_{1})=(1-\varepsilon)\tfrac12
=\tfrac12(1+\omega)\notin\mathfrak E.
\]

(b)  \emph{Now assume that all three vertices of the triangle are in
$\mathfrak E$.}  After translating by $-z_{0}\in\mathfrak E$ we may
suppose the first vertex is $0$.  If the first side-vector equals
$d\in\mathfrak E\setminus\{0\}$, the other vertices are
\[
0,\ d,\ \varepsilon d=-\omega d.
\]
Conversely any translated triangle in $\mathfrak E^{2}$ with first
side-vector $d$ must coincide with this one.  Hence, \emph{modulo
translations by $\mathfrak E$}, there is exactly one clockwise
equilateral triangle with prescribed non-zero
$d\in\mathfrak E$.

Let $U=\{\pm1,\pm\omega,\pm\omega^{2}\}$ be the unit group of
$\mathfrak E$.  Multiplication by $u\in U$ sends the representative
$\{0,d,\varepsilon d\}$ to $u\cdot\{0,d,\varepsilon d\}$, thereby
acting freely and transitively on the six possible first side-vectors
of the same $\mathfrak E$-norm.

\hfill$\square$



%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
Compared with the original “find the third vertex’’ question, the
enhanced variant

• elevates the geometry from a *single* equilateral triangle to the
   iterative dynamics that *generates an entire regular hexagon*;

• introduces linear–algebraic machinery: one must build and analyse a
   2×2 complex matrix, find its minimal polynomial, eigenvalues and
   order;

• requires bridging algebra and geometry (matrix powers ↔ rotations of
   edge-vectors) to prove regularity and locate the centre;

• adds a number-theoretic layer over the Gaussian integers, demanding a
   lattice/ideal test for integrality of all six vertices and a counting
   argument for equivalence classes of hexagons;

• compels the solver to juggle several interacting concepts—complex
   affine maps, root-of-unity rotations, linear recurrence, group order,
   lattice arithmetic—making it substantially more intricate than both
   the original problem and the current kernel variant.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let  
      \varepsilon  := e^{-\pi  i /3}= \frac{1}{2} -(\sqrt{3} /2)i ,   \rho  := \varepsilon -1 = e^{-2\pi  i /3}  
(so \rho ^3 = 1, \rho  \neq  1).  
For two distinct complex numbers (z_0 ,z_1) define the clockwise
equilateral transform  

  E(z_0 ,z_1) := (1-\varepsilon ) z_0 + \varepsilon  z_1.  (\star )

(The point E(z_0 ,z_1) is the unique third vertex that completes the
clockwise-oriented equilateral triangle on the ordered side z_0z_1.)

Introduce the linear map  

  \Phi  : \mathbb{C}^2 \to  \mathbb{C}^2,  \Phi (z_0 ,z_1) = (z_1 , E(z_0 ,z_1)).

Starting from a non-degenerate ordered edge (z_0 ,z_1) consider the
sequence of vertices  

  z_{n+1} = E(z_{\,n-1}, z_{\,n})  (n \geq  1).  ()

(The ordered pairs (z_{n-1},z_{n}) are obtained by iterating \Phi .)

Answer the following questions.

1.  Algebraic background  
    (a)  Prove that \Phi  is \mathbb{C}-linear and determine the matrix A of \Phi  in the
         standard basis of \mathbb{C}^2.  
    (b)  Compute the characteristic and the minimal polynomial of A,
         prove that A has the distinct eigenvalues 1 and \rho , deduce that
         A is diagonalisable and satisfies A^3 = I while
         A \neq  I, A^2 \neq  I.  Show that \Phi  therefore has exact order 3.

2.  Explicit dynamics  
    (a)  Put w := z_1-z_0.  Show that for every n \geq  0  
              z_n = z_0 + [(1-\rho ^n)/(1-\rho )] w.  
    (b)  Conclude that (z_n) is 3-periodic and that the orbit is
         constant iff z_1 = z_0.

3.  Geometry  
    (a)  Verify directly from (\star ) that  
         \Delta _0 := (z_0 ,z_1 ,z_2) is an equilateral triangle oriented
         clockwise.  
    (b)  Let  
             G := (z_0+z_1+z_2)/3  
         be its centroid.  Prove the fundamental rotation identity  

             z_{n+1} - G = \rho  ( z_n - G )  (n \geq  0),

         and deduce that \Phi  acts, on the level of single vertices, as the
         order-3 rotation about G through the angle arg \rho  = -120^\circ.  
         Determine, in terms of |w|,  
             * the circum-radius  R  and  
             * the in-radius     r  
         of \Delta _0.  (Show in particular that R \neq  r, although the two
         centres coincide.)  
    (c)  Conversely, show that every clockwise-oriented equilateral
         triangle in \mathbb{C} arises from a unique ordered side (z_0 ,z_1) via
         the scheme ().

4.  Arithmetic refinement over the Eisenstein integers  
   Let \omega  := e^{2\pi  i /3} and E := \mathbb{Z}[\omega ].

   (a)  Prove that \varepsilon  = -\omega  and 1-\varepsilon  = 1+\omega  both belong to E and deduce
        that E(E,E) \subset  E; hence for z_0 ,z_1 \in  E the whole 3-periodic
        orbit (z_0 ,z_1 ,z_2) lies in E.  Exhibit an explicit example
        with exactly one of z_0 ,z_1 in E for which z_2 \notin  E.  

   (b)  Fix a non-zero d \in  E.  Up to translation by elements of E,
        determine and count all clockwise-oriented equilateral triangles
        whose first side-vector equals d.  Explain how the unit group
        {\pm 1, \pm \omega , \pm \omega ^2} acts on this family.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Notation.  \varepsilon  = e^{-\pi  i /3}, \rho  := \varepsilon -1 = e^{-2\pi  i /3} (\rho ^3 = 1, \rho  \neq  1).


1.  Algebraic background  

(a)  The map (\star ) is \mathbb{C}-linear in each argument and homogeneous of degree
one, hence E itself is \mathbb{C}-bilinear; consequently \Phi , defined by
\Phi (z_0 ,z_1) = (z_1 ,E(z_0 ,z_1)), is \mathbb{C}-linear.  With column vectors
v = (z_0 ,z_1)^t we get  

  \Phi (v) = ( z_1 ,(1-\varepsilon )z_0 + \varepsilon  z_1 ) = A v with  

  A = [ 0 1; 1-\varepsilon  \varepsilon  ].  (1)

(b)  Characteristic polynomial  

 \chi _A(\lambda ) = det(\lambda I - A) = \lambda ^2 - \varepsilon  \lambda  - (1-\varepsilon ). (2)

Plugging \lambda  = 1 and \lambda  = \rho  gives \chi _A(1)=\chi _A(\rho )=0, so

 \chi _A(\lambda )= (\lambda -1)(\lambda -\rho ).  

The two roots are distinct; hence A is diagonalisable and its minimal
polynomial equals \chi _A.  As \rho ^3=1 we have \chi _A | (\lambda ^3-1), therefore A^3=I.
Because \rho  \neq  \pm 1, neither A nor A^2 is the identity, so \Phi  has exact
order 3.


2.  Explicit dynamics  

Let w := z_1-z_0 and v_n := (z_{\,n-1}, z_{\,n})^t (n \geq  1).
Then v_{n}=A^{n-1}v_1.  The spectral resolution of A is  

 A^{n}= P_1 + \rho ^{\,n} P_\rho ,  P_1+P_\rho =I, P_1P_\rho =0, (3)

where P_1, P_\rho  are the eigen-projectors.  Applying (3) to v_1 and taking
the second coordinate yields  

 z_n = z_0 + [(1-\rho ^{\,n})/(1-\rho )] w,  n \geq  0. (4)

(b)  Because \rho ^3 = 1, the right-hand side is 3-periodic.  If some
z_{n+1}=z_n then the multiplier of w in (4) vanishes, forcing w=0,
i.e. z_1=z_0.  Thus the orbit is constant exactly in the degenerate case;
otherwise it has minimal period 3.


3.  Geometry  

(a)  From (\star )

 z_2-z_0 = \varepsilon  w,  z_2-z_1 = \rho  w,

hence all sides have length |w| and the oriented angle from z_1-z_0 to
z_2-z_1 equals arg \rho  = -120^\circ, i.e. clockwise.  Thus \Delta _0 is equilateral.

(b)  Put G := (z_0+z_1+z_2)/3.  
Applying (4) for n = 0,1,2 we get  

 z_1-G = \rho  (z_0-G),  z_2-G = \rho  (z_1-G),

and multiplying by \rho  iteratively gives for all n \geq  0  

 z_{n+1} - G = \rho  ( z_n - G ). (5)

Hence \Phi  acts as the rotation R_{G,-120^\circ}.

Circum- and in-radii.  
Because all vertices are at the same distance from G, the circum-radius
is  

 R = |z_0-G| = |w|/\sqrt{3.}  

For an equilateral triangle, the in-radius satisfies r = R/2, so  

 r = |w|/(2\sqrt{3}).  

Thus R \neq  r, although both centres coincide at G.

(c)  Conversely, let T be any clockwise equilateral triangle with
ordered side (p,q).  By construction E(p,q) is the unique vertex r that
completes T, and iterating () reproduces T.  Different ordered sides
give different triangles, so the correspondence is bijective.


4.  Arithmetic refinement over E = \mathbb{Z}[\omega ]  

(a)  We have \varepsilon  = -\omega  and 1-\varepsilon  = 1+\omega  \in  E, so E(E,E) \subset  E and any orbit
issued from z_0 ,z_1 \in  E stays in E by 3-periodicity.
Counter-example with only one entry in E: take  
 z_0 = \frac{1}{2} \notin  E,  z_1 = 0 \in  E.  
Then  

 z_2 = E(z_0 ,z_1) = (1-\varepsilon )\cdot \frac{1}{2} + \varepsilon \cdot 0 = (1+\omega )/2 \notin  E,

so the orbit leaves E.

(b)  Translate so that the first vertex is 0.  With side-vector d \neq  0
the triangle is {0, d, \varepsilon  d}= {0, d, -\omega  d}, which lies in E by (a) and
is clockwise.  Any other translate differs by an element of E, hence
modulo translations there is exactly one such triangle.

Let U = {\pm 1, \pm \omega , \pm \omega ^2} be the unit group of E.  Multiplying the fixed
side-vector d by a unit u sends the triangle {0,d,\varepsilon  d} to
{0,ud, \varepsilon (ud)} = u\cdot {0,d,\varepsilon  d}.  Thus U acts freely and transitively on
the family of admissible side-vectors of the same norm, while the set
of triangles themselves consists of a single orbit modulo translations.
